Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Visual Perception Visual display design rests on three pillars of human vision science: ### Pillar 1 — Optical Limits of the Eye - The eye's resolving power is limited by the **angular size** of an object, not its physical size. - A feature subtending less than **1 minute of arc** cannot be distinguished from a point. ### Pillar 2 — Photometric Sensitivity - The eye responds logarithmically to luminance — equal *ratios* of luminance are perceived as equal *steps* of brightness (Weber–Fechner Law). - Therefore, **contrast ratios** (not absolute differences) govern legibility. ### Pillar 3 — Cognitive Processing Speed - Symbols that match learned patterns (familiar digits, standard icons) are recognized faster than novel shapes. - This underlies the rule: **standardized fonts (e.g., ISO 9241)** outperform decorative fonts in operator displays. ## 2.2 Detailed Design Logic — Step-by-Step ### Step 1 — Identify the Display Function - Is it *quantitative*, *qualitative*, or *check*? → dictates format. - How frequently is it read? (continuous vs. occasional) ### Step 2 — Determine the Required Visual Angle - For *critical reading*: minimum character height subtends **16 to 22 minutes of arc** at the eye. - For *incidental reading*: minimum **12 to 18 minutes of arc**. - For *degraded conditions* (vibration, low light, brief glances): **30 to 40 minutes of arc**. ### Step 3 — Compute Physical Character Height - Apply the legibility equation (derived in §3.1): $$H = D \cdot \tan(\theta)$$ - For typical workstation $D = 0.7$ m and $\theta = 20′$: $$H = 0.7 \cdot \tan(20 / 60 \cdot \pi / 180) \approx 4.07 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 4 — Specify Contrast and Luminance - Display-to-background luminance ratio must satisfy $CR \geq 3:1$ (minimum) and ideally $CR \geq 10:1$ (recommended). - Surround luminance should be **0.5× to 1×** the display luminance to avoid re-adaptation fatigue. ### Step 5 — Check Viewing Geometry - Vertical viewing angle: **0° to 30° below horizontal line of sight** is preferred. - Horizontal viewing angle: within **±30°** of the operator's forward gaze. ### Step 6 — Anti-Glare & Reflection Control - Use **matte (anti-reflection) coatings**, hoods, or polarizers. - Eliminate specular reflections from ambient light sources. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are mathematically verifiable from the physics of optics and standardized in ISO 9241-300 series for ergonomic display design.** | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variable Definitions | Typical Range | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Legibility (Visual Angle) | $\theta = \arctan(H/D) \cdot 60$ | $\theta$ in arc-minutes, $H$ = character height, $D$ = viewing distance | 12′ to 40′ | | 2 | Character Height from Viewing Distance | $H = D \cdot \tan(\theta/60 \cdot \pi/180)$ | same as above | 3 to 30 mm | | 3 | Snellen Acuity Criterion | $\theta_{min} = 1′$ | minimum resolvable angle | constant | | 4 | Contrast Ratio | $CR = L_{max} / L_{min}$ | $L_{max}$ = peak luminance, $L_{min}$ = background luminance | $\geq 3:1$ | | 5 | Weber's Fraction (Luminance) | $\Delta L / L = k$ | $k \approx 0.01$ to $0.02$ | constant for mesopic vision | | 6 | Stevens' Power Law (Brightness) | $S = k \cdot L^{0.33}$ | $S$ = perceived brightness, $L$ = luminance, exponent 0.33 | n/a | | 7 | Character Width Ratio | $W = (0.5 \text{ to } 1.0) \cdot H$ | alphanumeric width-to-height | ratio | | 8 | Stroke Width | $s = (1/8 \text{ to } 1/6) \cdot H$ | stroke width for legibility | ratio | | 9 | Character Spacing | $P_c = (0.5 \text{ to } 1.0) \cdot H$ | spacing between characters | ratio | | 10 | Line Spacing | $P_l = (1.0 \text{ to } 2.0) \cdot H$ | spacing between text rows | ratio | | 11 | Display Frame Rate | $f_r \geq 1 / (2 \cdot T_{persistence})$ | $T_{persistence}$ = phosphor/panel decay time | $\geq 60$ Hz | | 12 | Optimal Viewing Distance (Monitor) | $D = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.5) \cdot D_{screen}$ | $D_{screen}$ = diagonal screen size | ratio | | 13 | Pixels Per Character | $N_{px} \geq 7 \times 9$ | minimum for legible alphanumeric | integer | | 14 | Display Aspect Ratio | $AR = W/H$ | width-by-height of screen | 4:3, 16:9, 16:10 | | 15 | Field of View Coverage | $FOV = 2 \cdot \arctan(d / 2D)$ | $d$ = screen width | degrees | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Engineering Utility**: These formulas are applied in the design of **aviation cockpit displays** (FAR 25.773), **automotive instrument clusters** (ISO 15005), **medical imaging monitors** (IEC 62563-1), and **industrial control panels** (ISA 101.01). Mastery of these equations is essential for the KTU 14-mark design problems. ## 2.4 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why" Behind the Math - **Step 1 (Visual Angle):** The eye cannot be enlarged; hence information must be **scaled to the eye**, not the eye to the information. - **Step 2 (Character Height):** Designing for 20 minutes of arc ensures **99% legibility** for trained operators under normal lighting. - **Step 3 (Contrast):** Insufficient contrast is the **single largest cause** of VDT-induced visual fatigue (a known occupational health issue). - **Step 4 (Viewing Geometry):** The natural downward gaze angle aligns with the eye's **resting line of sight** (~15° below horizontal), minimizing neck strain. - **Step 5 (Refresh Rate):** Below ~50 Hz, the human eye perceives **flicker**, causing headaches and reduced detection accuracy. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Character-Height-to-Viewing-Distance Relationship ### Statement For a visual display, the minimum physical character height $H$ required for legibility at a given viewing distance $D$ and a required visual angle $\theta$ (in arc-minutes) is: $$H = D \cdot \tan(\theta / 60 \cdot \pi / 180)$$ ### Derivation (Exhaustive, Step-by-Step) **Step 1 — Start from the definition of visual angle.** The visual angle $\theta$ subtended by an object of physical height $H$ at distance $D$ from the eye is given by the small-angle trigonometric definition: $$\theta_{\text{rad}} = \arctan(H / D)$$ **Step 2 — Convert the angle to minutes of arc.** Since the problem specifies $\theta$ in arc-minutes, we apply the conversion factor $1$ radian $= (180/\pi) \cdot 60 = 3437.75$ arc-minutes: $$\theta_{\text{arcmin}} = \theta_{\text{rad}} \cdot (180/\pi) \cdot 60 = \theta_{\text{rad}} \cdot 3437.75$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $\theta_{\text{rad}}$ in terms of $\theta_{\text{arcmin}}$.** $$\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{arcmin}} / 3437.75 = \theta_{\text{arcmin}} / (60 \cdot 180/\pi) = \theta_{\text{arcmin}} \cdot \pi / (60 \cdot 180)$$ Substituting into Step 1: $$\theta_{\text{arcmin}} \cdot \pi / (60 \cdot 180) = \arctan(H / D)$$ **Step 4 — Take the tangent of both sides.** Since for small angles $\arctan(x) \approx x$ (and here $\theta$ is small enough to justify this with negligible error for engineering purposes), we obtain the working approximation: $$H / D \approx \theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{arcmin}} \cdot \pi / (60 \cdot 180)$$ **Step 5 — Multiply both sides by $D$ to isolate $H$.** $$\boxed{H = D \cdot \theta_{\text{arcmin}} \cdot \pi / (60 \cdot 180) = D \cdot \tan(\theta_{\text{arcmin}} \cdot \pi / 10800)}$$ This is the **canonical legibility equation** used in KTU-validated ergonomics textbooks. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Pattern **Problem:** A control-room operator reads a digital display from a viewing distance of **800 mm**. The ergonomic standard requires that each digit subtend a visual angle of **22 minutes of arc** at the eye. Calculate the minimum character height. **Solution:** Given: $D = 800$ mm, $\theta = 22′$. Apply the equation derived in §3.1: $$H = D \cdot \tan(\theta \cdot \pi / 10800)$$ Compute the argument of the tangent in radians: $$\theta \cdot \pi / 10800 = 22 \cdot 3.14159 / 10800 = 69.115 / 10800 = 6.3996 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$ Evaluate the tangent (for such a small angle, $\tan(x) \approx x$): $$\tan(6.3996 \times 10^{-3}) \approx 6.3996 \times 10^{-3}$$ Multiply by $D$: $$H = 800 \cdot 6.3996 \times 10^{-3} = 5.1197 \text{ mm} \approx 5.12 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{H_{\min} \approx 5.12 \text{ mm}}$$ **Verification (alternate route via small-angle approximation):** Using $H \approx D \cdot \theta_{rad}$: $$\theta_{rad} = 22 / 3437.75 = 6.3996 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$ $$H = 800 \times 6.3996 \times 10^{-3} = 5.12 \text{ mm}$$ **Result confirmed.** The minimum digit height should be **at least 5.12 mm** to satisfy the 22 arc-minute legibility criterion at 800 mm viewing distance. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Contrast Ratio for Display Background ### Statement The contrast ratio $CR$ of a display element against its background is defined as the ratio of the peak luminance of the active element to the luminance of the background. ### Derivation **Step 1 — Define the luminances.** Let $L_a$ = luminance of the active (foreground) element, $L_b$ = luminance of the background. Both measured in $\text{cd/m}^2$. **Step 2 — Define contrast ratio by convention.** The ANSI/IES standard defines: $$CR = L_a / L_b$$ provided $L_a > L_b$ (positive polarity — black text on white background is the most readable). **Step 3 — Inverse-polarity correction.** For light text on dark background (common in aviation), swap the numerator: $$CR_{inv} = L_b / L_a$$ **Step 4 — Numerical illustration.** Suppose $L_a = 200 \text{ cd/m}^2$ and $L_b = 20 \text{ cd/m}^2$: $$CR = 200 / 20 = 10:1$$ This exceeds the ergonomic minimum of $3:1$ and meets the recommended value of $10:1$. ## 3.4 Derivation of Refresh Rate from Phosphor Persistence ### Statement For a CRT-type display, the refresh rate $f_r$ must satisfy: $$f_r \geq 1 / (2 \cdot T_p)$$ where $T_p$ is the phosphor persistence time (afterglow). ### Derivation **Step 1 — Define flicker perception.** The human eye perceives flicker when the time between successive refreshes exceeds the persistence of vision (approximately **50 ms**, the **critical flicker frequency (CFF)** threshold under typical display luminance). **Step 2 — CFF condition.** The critical flicker frequency in Hz is: $$CFF \approx 1 / (2 \cdot T_p)$$ **Step 3 — Design rule.** To eliminate flicker perception: $$f_r \geq 1 / (2 \cdot T_p)$$ **Step 4 — Numerical example.** If $T_p = 8$ ms (typical for modern LCD equivalent): $$f_r \geq 1 / (0.016) = 62.5 \text{ Hz}$$ Hence a refresh rate of **60 Hz or higher** is required. Modern displays use **75, 120, 144, or 240 Hz** for added comfort. ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Display Selection | Application | Recommended Display Type | Critical Parameter | Reason | |---|---|---|---| | Aircraft cockpit | Active-matrix LCD / OLED | Luminance $\geq 200 \text{ cd/m}^2$, sunlight readability | High ambient light | | Automobile dashboard | Transflective LCD | Wide viewing angle ($\pm 60°$), $CR \geq 5:1$ | Driver glances, motion | | Medical imaging | IPS / OLED medical-grade | Resolution $\geq 3$ MP, calibrated color | Diagnostic accuracy | | Industrial control panel | LED segmented | Refresh rate $\geq 60$ Hz, $CR \geq 7:1$ | 24/7 operation | | Consumer TV | OLED / QLED | $CR \geq 1000:1$, HDR support | Entertainment quality | | Mobile device | AMOLED | PPI $\geq 400$, peak $L \geq 600 \text{ cd/m}^2$ | Portability, outdoor use | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification Block Diagram — Visual Displays The following Mermaid diagram presents a **functional classification architecture** of visual displays as per KTU Module 4 syllabus, mapping the relationship between display types, sub-types, and their typical application domains. ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode([Visual Display Devices]) --> classBranch{Classification Basis} classBranch --> type1[By Information Type] classBranch --> type2[By Technology] classBranch --> type3[By Presentation Mode] type1 --> qd[Quantitative Displays] type1 --> qld[Qualitative Displays] type1 --> cd[Check Displays] qd --> pointerInstr[Pointer on Scale] qd --> digitalRead[Digital Numeric Readout] qd --> counterRead[Counter Type Display] qld --> barGraph[Bar Graphs] qld --> trendPlot[Trend Recorders] qld --> oscilloscope[Oscilloscope Traces] qld --> radarPlot[Radar PPI Display] cd --> pilotLamp[Pilot Lamps ON OFF] cd --> flagInd[Flag Indicators] cd --> iconStat[Icon Status Indicators] type2 --> techCRT[Cathode Ray Tube CRT] type2 --> techLCD[Liquid Crystal Display LCD] type2 --> techLED[Light Emitting Diode LED] type2 --> techOLED[Organic LED OLED] type2 --> techProj[Projection Displays] type2 --> techHUD[Head Up Display HUD] type3 --> modeStatic[Static Displays] type3 --> modeDynamic[Dynamic Displays] type3 --> modeMultimodal[Multimodal Displays] modeStatic --> labelSign[Warning Labels Signs] modeStatic --> panelMark[Permanent Panel Markings] modeDynamic --> movingDial[Moving Pointer Meters] modeDynamic --> scrollingTxt[Scrolling Text VDT] modeDynamic --> realTimeVid[Real Time Video Feeds] modeMultimodal --> touchScr[Touch Screen Haptic] modeMultimodal --> audioVisual[Audio Visual Integrated] classDef default fill:E8F4FD,stroke:1F4E79,color:000 classDef typeNode fill:FFF2CC,stroke:B7950B,color:000 classDef appNode fill:D5F5E3,stroke:1E8449,color:000 class startNode type1 type2 type3 typeNode class pointerInstr digitalRead counterRead barGraph trendPlot oscilloscope radarPlot pilotLamp flagInd iconStat techCRT techLCD techLED techOLED techProj techHUD modeStatic modeDynamic modeMultimodal labelSign panelMark movingDial scrollingTxt realTimeVid touchScr audioVisual appNode ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture Block — Visual Display System ```mermaid flowchart LR inNode([Input Signal
Electrical Mechanical]) --> procNode[Signal Processor
Controller] procNode --> driveNode[Display Driver
Electronics] driveNode --> panelNode[Display Panel
Luminescent Surface] panelNode --> lightNode[Luminous Pattern
Image Formation] lightNode --> eyeNode([Human Eye
Retina Optical System]) eyeNode --> brainNode([Visual Cortex
Perception Interpretation]) brainNode --> actNode([Operator Action
Decision Response]) actNode --> inNode panelNode -.luminance.-> lcheck{Luminance
Contrast Check} panelNode -.geometry.-> gcheck{Viewing Angle
Distance Check} lcheck -->|fail| recalibNode[Recalibrate Drive] gcheck -->|fail| recalibNode recalibNode --> driveNode classDef flow fill:E8F4FD,stroke:1F4E79,color:000 classDef check fill:FCF3CF,stroke:B7950B,color:000 classDef ctrl fill:FADBD8,stroke:C0392B,color:000 class inNode eyeNode brainNode actNode flow class lcheck gcheck check class recalibNode ctrl ``` ## 4.3 Character Geometry Reference (Conceptual) > [!NOTE] > **Note on physical character proportions** (commonly tested in KTU 14-mark questions): | Dimension | Ratio to Character Height $H$ | |---|---| | Character Width (alphanumeric) | $W = 0.5 H$ to $1.0 H$ | | Stroke Width | $s = (1/8) H$ to $(1/6) H$ | | Inter-Character Spacing | $P_c = 0.5 H$ to $1.0 H$ | | Inter-Word Spacing | $P_w = 1.0 H$ to $1.5 H$ | | Inter-Line Spacing | $P_l = 1.0 H$ to $2.0 H$ | The **ideal Snellen-compliant capital letter** (e.g., the letter "E") has: - Total height = $H$ - Total width = $0.7 H$ - Horizontal stroke count = 3 - Stroke width = $H/8$ A display that violates these proportions (e.g., condensed sans-serif fonts at small sizes) increases cognitive load and reading errors by **up to 40%** (Sanders & McCormick, *Human Factors in Engineering and Design*, classical reference). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a visual display. Differentiate between a quantitative display and a check display with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **visual display** is a device or surface that presents information to the human operator in the form of luminous or contrast patterns that can be perceived by the eye and interpreted cognitively. - **Quantitative display**: Conveys precise numerical magnitudes. *Example*: a digital speedometer reading "67 km/h" or a pointer moving over a calibrated scale on an analog voltmeter. - **Check display**: Conveys only the *state* or *condition* of a system, typically binary or multi-state. *Example*: a green pilot lamp indicating "Power ON" or a red warning light indicating "System Fault". The key distinction: a quantitative display answers *"how much / how many"*, whereas a check display answers *"on/off / safe/unsafe / normal/abnormal"*. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State Snellen's standard of visual acuity. Why is it relevant to the design of visual displays?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Snellen's standard of visual acuity** states that a person with normal (20/20) vision can resolve a detail that subtends a **visual angle of 1 minute of arc (1′)** at the eye, under high-contrast, well-lit conditions. **Relevance to display design:** 1. It establishes the **minimum angular size** of any character, symbol, or marker that must appear on a display for it to be reliably resolvable by a typical operator. 2. Designers use this threshold (often multiplied by safety factors of **10 to 25 times**, giving design targets of **10′ to 25′**) to compute the required physical character height at any given viewing distance using the legibility equation. 3. It underpins all ergonomic standards such as **ISO 9241-300**, **ANSI/HFES 100**, and **MIL-STD-1472** for military and industrial displays. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Module Pattern**: Each 14-mark question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You must attempt **one full question** (either Q. A or Q. B) but **not both**. Sub-parts are mandatory and complementary. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **(a) Classify visual displays into three functional categories. Explain with neat sketches the working principle of (i) a moving-pointer analog display and (ii) a seven-segment LED numeric display. Discuss one ergonomic advantage and one disadvantage of each. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Classification of Visual Displays:** 1. **Quantitative displays** — convey precise numerical values (analog dials, digital readouts). 2. **Qualitative displays** — convey trends, rates, or relative magnitudes (bar graphs, oscilloscope traces). 3. **Check (status) displays** — convey binary or discrete states (pilot lamps, indicator flags). **(i) Moving-Pointer Analog Display:** A pointer attached to a calibrated scale rotates proportionally to the magnitude of the measured variable. The reading is taken where the pointer intersects the scale markings. - **Ergonomic Advantage**: Provides **continuous, at-a-glance trend perception** — the operator can detect the *direction* and *rate of change* faster than reading a digital number, leveraging the eye's superior motion sensitivity. - **Disadvantage**: Prone to **reading parallax errors** if the pointer is viewed from an off-axis angle; less precise than digital readouts for exact values. **(ii) Seven-Segment LED Display:** Seven individual LED bars (labeled a through g) are selectively illuminated in patterns corresponding to digits 0–9. Each digit is encoded via a decoder circuit (BCD to 7-segment). - **Ergonomic Advantage**: Provides **unambiguous numerical readout** with high contrast; readable in low-light environments; no parallax error. - **Disadvantage**: Only **numeric** information (limited alphanumeric capability in standard form); characters are formed from straight segments only, reducing aesthetic legibility compared to true fonts. **(b) The luminance of a visual display character is 250 cd/m² and the background is 30 cd/m². Compute the contrast ratio. If the operator views the display from 600 mm, and the required visual angle for legibility is 18 arc-minutes, calculate the minimum character height in mm. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute Contrast Ratio (CR).** $$CR = L_{max} / L_{min} = 250 / 30$$ $$\boxed{CR = 8.33 : 1}$$ Since $CR \geq 3:1$ and approaches the recommended $10:1$, the contrast is **acceptable** for ergonomic use. **Step 2 — Compute Minimum Character Height.** Given: $D = 600$ mm, $\theta = 18′$. Using the legibility equation (derived in §3.1): $$H = D \cdot \tan(\theta \cdot \pi / 10800)$$ Compute the argument: $$\theta \cdot \pi / 10800 = 18 \cdot 3.14159 / 10800 = 56.549 / 10800 = 5.2359 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$ Evaluate tangent (small-angle approximation valid): $$\tan(5.2359 \times 10^{-3}) \approx 5.2359 \times 10^{-3}$$ Multiply by $D$: $$H = 600 \cdot 5.2359 \times 10^{-3} = 3.1415 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{H_{\min} \approx 3.14 \text{ mm}}$$ **Step 3 — Recommend stroke width and spacing (synthesis).** - Stroke width $s = H/8$ to $H/6$ → $s \approx 0.39$ to $0.52$ mm. - Character spacing $P_c = 0.5 H$ to $1.0 H$ → $P_c \approx 1.57$ to $3.14$ mm. The display is therefore **ergonomically compliant** if these dimensional ratios are maintained. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) With the aid of a labelled diagram, explain the working of a Head-Up Display (HUD). Discuss its ergonomic advantages over conventional head-down displays in aviation. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Working of a Head-Up Display (HUD):** A Head-Up Display projects critical flight information (airspeed, altitude, heading, attitude, navigation cues) onto a **semi-transparent combiner glass** located between the pilot and the forward windshield. The optical path consists of: 1. **Symbol Generator** — produces the electronic image of flight data. 2. **Display Unit (CRT/LCD source)** — emits the luminous image. 3. **Relay Optics / Combiner** — collimates the image to optical infinity and superimposes it on the external view via partial reflection. 4. **Pilot's Eye Position (Design Eye Reference Point — DEP)** — fixed viewing location where the collimated image is in focus simultaneously with the distant runway scene. The image is **collimated** (appears at infinity), so the pilot's eyes do not need to re-accommodate when shifting gaze between the HUD symbology and the outside world. **Ergonomic Advantages of HUD over Head-Down Displays (HDD):** 1. **Reduced eye-re accommodation time** — pilot does not refocus between near display and far runway, saving critical **~1 second** during landing. 2. **Enhanced situational awareness** — flight data is overlaid on the external scene, enabling simultaneous monitoring. 3. **Improved safety in poor visibility** — guidance cues remain visible even when the runway is obscured. 4. **Reduced head and neck strain** — no need to look down at instrument panel. **Disadvantages:** - Limited field of view (typically 30° × 24°). - Higher cost and complexity. - Potential for **visual clutter / cognitive overload** if poorly designed. **(b) Discuss the ergonomic guidelines for designing alphanumeric displays for operator readability. Your answer must include formulas for character height, stroke width, and spacing. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Ergonomic Guidelines for Alphanumeric Displays:** 1. **Character Height ($H$):** Determined by viewing distance $D$ and required visual angle $\theta$: $$H = D \cdot \tan(\theta \cdot \pi / 10800)$$ - Minimum for normal reading: $\theta = 16′$ to $22′$. - For critical/emergency reading: $\theta = 30′$ to $40′$. 2. **Stroke Width ($s$):** $$s = (1/8 \text{ to } 1/6) \cdot H$$ A stroke too thin (below $H/10$) appears to vanish; too thick (above $H/5$) causes "blobbing". 3. **Character Width ($W$):** $$W = (0.5 \text{ to } 1.0) \cdot H$$ Most legible: $W \approx 0.7 H$ (close to Snellen's "E" geometry). 4. **Character Spacing ($P_c$):** $$P_c = (0.5 \text{ to } 1.0) \cdot H$$ Tight spacing below $0.5 H$ causes "letter crowding"; loose spacing above $1.0 H$ slows reading. 5. **Line Spacing ($P_l$):** $$P_l = (1.0 \text{ to } 2.0) \cdot H$$ Preferably $1.5 H$ for body text. 6. **Contrast Ratio ($CR$):** $$CR = L_{max} / L_{min} \geq 3:1 \text{ (minimum)}, \quad \geq 10:1 \text{ (recommended)}$$ 7. **Font Selection:** Sans-serif (e.g., ISO 9241, DIN, Highway Gothic) outperforms serif fonts on VDTs. 8. **Viewing Angle:** Perpendicular to display surface, within $\pm 15°$ for optimum legibility. **Sample Design Calculation:** For $D = 700$ mm and $\theta = 20′$: - $H = 700 \cdot \tan(20 \cdot \pi/10800) = 700 \cdot 5.82 \times 10^{-3} \approx 4.07$ mm - $s = H/8 \approx 0.51$ mm - $W = 0.7 H \approx 2.85$ mm - $P_c = 0.75 H \approx 3.05$ mm **Verification:** A character 4 mm tall, viewed from 700 mm, subtends approximately $20′$ — fully compliant with ISO 9241-300 series. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Mixing mm and m in the legibility equation. Always keep $D$ and $H$ in the **same unit** before substitution. > 2. **Angle Unit Mix-up**: Forgetting to convert arc-minutes to radians before computing $\tan$. The factor is $\pi / (60 \cdot 180) = \pi / 10800$. > 3. **Contrast Ratio Polarity**: Students often write $CR$ without specifying whether it is positive (dark on light) or inverse (light on dark) polarity. Always state the convention. > 4. **Confusing CFF with Refresh Rate**: CFF (Critical Flicker Frequency) is a *physiological limit* of the eye (~50 Hz under typical luminance); refresh rate must exceed $2 \cdot T_p^{-1}$, NOT necessarily equal CFF. > 5. **Skipping the "Why"**: KTU awards 2–3 marks per sub-question for *justification*, not just numerical answers. Always state the **engineering rationale** behind your choice of $\theta$, $CR$, or viewing distance. > 6. **Confusing HUD with HDD**: Head-Up Display projects at optical infinity; Head-Down Display is viewed at close range. Do not interchange these terms. > 7. **Snellen Quotient Misapplication**: Snellen's 1′ is the *limit of resolution*, not the *design target*. Always use a multiplier (typically 10–25×) for actual displays. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4** - **Visual Display Definition**: A device converting signals into luminous patterns for human perception. - **Three Functional Categories**: Quantitative (numeric), Qualitative (trends), Check (status). - **Snellen's Acuity**: 1 minute of arc is the **physiological minimum** resolvable detail. - **Legibility Design Target**: **16′ to 22′** for normal reading; **30′ to 40′** for critical/emergency. - **Master Equation**: $H = D \cdot \tan(\theta \cdot \pi / 10800)$. - **Contrast Ratio (CR)**: $L_{max} / L_{min}$, must be $\geq 3:1$, recommended $\geq 10:1$. - **Weber's Fraction**: $\Delta L / L \approx 0.01$ to $0.02$ for mesopic vision. - **Stevens' Power Law**: Brightness perception scales as $L^{0.33}$. - **Stroke Width**: $s = H/8$ to $H/6$. - **Character Width**: $W = 0.5 H$ to $1.0 H$ (ideal $0.7 H$). - **Spacing Rules**: Character $P_c = 0.5 H$ to $1.0 H$; Word $P_w = 1.0 H$ to $1.5 H$; Line $P_l = 1.0 H$ to $2.0 H$. - **Refresh Rate**: $f_r \geq 1/(2 \cdot T_p)$, typically $\geq 60$ Hz. - **Optimal Viewing Distance**: $D = (1.5$ to $2.5) \times$ screen diagonal. - **Vertical Viewing Angle**: 0° to 30° below horizontal line of sight. - **Horizontal Viewing Angle**: within $\pm 30°$ of forward gaze. - **Display Technologies**: CRT (legacy), LCD (active/passive matrix), LED, OLED, projection, HUD. - **HUD Principle**: Projects collimated image at infinity, reducing eye-re accommodation time. - **Alphanumeric Standard**: Minimum **7 × 9 pixel** matrix per character for legibility. - **Aspect Ratios**: 4:3 (legacy), 16:10, 16:9 (modern widescreen). - **Anti-Glare**: Matte coatings, polarizers, hoods; eliminate specular reflections. - **Surround Luminance**: Should be **0.5× to 1×** of display luminance. - **Applicable Standards**: ISO 9241-300, ANSI/HFES 100, MIL-STD-1472, IEC 62563-1, FAR 25.773. - **Primary Failure Mode**: Insufficient contrast + small character height + poor viewing geometry → operator error. - **Design Mantra**: **"Scale information to the eye, not the eye to the information."**

topics.title: Design of Audio Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT864 - ERGONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Audio Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Audio Display:** An audio display (also termed *auditory display* or *acoustic display*) is a human-machine interface element that conveys information, status, or control feedback to the user through **non-speech sound signals** (tones, bursts, melodies) or **speech signals** (recorded or synthesized voice messages), engaging the auditory sensory channel of the human information-processing system.

In KTU 2024 Scheme ergonomics terminology, audio displays are classified under the broader category of **non-visual displays** and are formally defined by the **ISO 9241-200** and **ANSI S3.100** standards as devices or signals that exploit human auditory perception characteristics to communicate operational data, alarms, or status.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Audio displays are essential when (a) the operator's visual channel is overloaded, (b) the operator must move freely in the workspace, (c) immediate response is critical (e.g., alarms), or (d) the environment is dark or visually obscured.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Think of an **automobile dashboard**:

- When you press the indicator stalk, you *hear* a rhythmic **"tick-tick-tick"** — that is an audio display (a non-speech auditory icon).
- When the fuel is low, a continuous **"beep-beep"** warns you without you needing to look at the gauge.
- Modern cars even *speak* ("Door is open") — this is a **speech display**.

Just as a traffic policeman uses a **whistle** (non-speech) and **shouted verbal commands** (speech) to direct traffic, audio displays convert machine data into two broad kinds of acoustic outputs — abstract tones and natural language.

> [!TIP]
> **Why use sound?** Because vision is a "fixed gaze" channel — eyes must be pointed at the display. Hearing is an "ambient, omnidirectional" channel — it works in the dark, across a room, and while hands and eyes are busy. This is the **core ergonomic advantage** of audio displays.

## 1.3 The Four Fundamental Parameters of an Audio Signal

Any auditory stimulus used in a display can be decomposed into four psychoacoustic dimensions, each of which the designer can manipulate to encode information:

| Parameter | Physical Quantity | Typical Range for Displays | Perceptual Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency (Pitch)** | Hertz (Hz) | 250 Hz – 4 kHz | Encodes *what kind* of event |
| **Amplitude (Loudness)** | Decibels (dB SPL) | 10–20 dB above ambient | Encodes *urgency / importance* |
| **Duration** | Seconds (s) | 0.1 s – 2.0 s | Encodes *priority / transition* |
| **Timbre / Spectrum** | Harmonic content | Mixed pure tones | Encodes *identity of source* |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU pitfall is treating the four parameters as independent — in human perception, **pitch and loudness interact** (the equal-loudness contours of Fletcher-Munson are non-flat), and a signal buried 10 dB below noise may still be intelligible to a trained ear but completely masked for a novice.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Audio Displays (Overview)

```
AUDIO DISPLAYS
├── 1. Non-Speech Audio Displays (Signals)
│       ├── Auditory Icons (everyday sounds)
│       ├── Earcons (abstract, structured tones)
│       └── Audification (mapped data sonification)
└── 2. Speech Audio Displays
        ├── Recorded (pre-stored messages)
        └── Synthesized (text-to-speech, TTS)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auditory Icons** (term coined by Gaver, 1986) are *naturalistic* sounds that resemble the event they signal (e.g., a *paper-tearing* sound for "delete"). **Earcons** (Blattner et al., 1989) are *abstract* — they use musical motifs to represent operations (e.g., three ascending notes for "level up"). Both are valid; the choice depends on whether a *natural mapping* exists.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Human Auditory System — Operational Boundaries

The human ear responds to sound pressure waves across roughly **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz**, but the *useful range for audio displays* is much narrower because:

1. **Sensitivity is frequency-dependent** — the ear is most sensitive around 1–4 kHz.
2. **Background noise in industrial settings** typically has most energy below 500 Hz, so display tones should be tuned *above* this band for audibility.
3. **Aging** (presbycusis) reduces high-frequency hearing first — above 4 kHz, signal clarity degrades for older operators.

The reference sound pressure used universally is:

$$p_{ref} = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} \quad (= 0.00002 \, \text{Pa})$$

Sound Pressure Level (SPL) is defined as:

$$L_p = 20 \, \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_{ref}}\right) \quad \text{[dB SPL]}$$

where $p$ is the rms sound pressure of the signal.

## 2.2 Audibility Threshold & Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

> [!NOTE]
> For an audio display to be reliably detected, its level must exceed the *effective masked threshold*, which is the ambient noise level adjusted upward by a **critical masking band** phenomenon.

The minimum audibility requirement is:

$$\boxed{L_{signal} - L_{noise} \geq \Delta L_{min}}$$

where $\Delta L_{min}$ depends on the signal type:

- **Pure tones:** $\Delta L_{min} \approx \mathbf{3 \text{ dB}}$
- **Complex signals:** $\Delta L_{min} \approx \mathbf{6 \text{ dB}}$
- **Speech (intelligibility):** $\Delta L_{min} \approx \mathbf{10 \text{–} 15 \text{ dB}}$

## 2.3 Masking Phenomenon

Masking occurs when the perception of one sound (the *signal*) is impaired by the simultaneous presence of another sound (the *masker*). In practical audio-display engineering, masking is the **single most common reason** displays fail in the field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Band Theory (Zwicker, 1961):** The ear acts as a bank of band-pass filters (the *basilar membrane* critical bands), each ~⅓ octave wide. A tone masks another only if both fall within the same critical band. The effective masking level is:

$$L_{masked} = L_{masker} + \text{(Critical band offset, typically } +3 \text{ to } +24 \text{ dB)}$$

**Practical rule:** To make a display audible in broadband industrial noise, place its center frequency in the **2–4 kHz** region, where masking is least severe and ear sensitivity is highest.

## 2.4 Speech Intelligibility — Articulation Index (AI)

For speech audio displays, simple SNR is insufficient. The **Articulation Index (AI)**, later refined as the **Speech Intelligibility Index (SII)** under ANSI S3.5-1997, computes a weighted sum across frequency bands:

$$\boxed{AI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot \left(\frac{S_i - N_i + 15}{30}\right)}$$

where:

- $W_i$ = **band importance function** for the $i^{th}$ octave band (peaks at 2 kHz for English speech, $W \approx 0.18$).
- $S_i$ = speech spectrum level (dB) in band $i$.
- $N_i$ = noise spectrum level (dB) in band $i$.
- The "+15" / "30" constants normalise the contribution to lie in $[0, 1]$.

**Interpretation table:**

| AI Value | Intelligibility Rating |
|---|---|
| $AI \geq 0.7$ | Excellent — operators will understand 100% of words |
| $0.5 \leq AI < 0.7$ | Good — 80–95% intelligibility, acceptable |
| $0.3 \leq AI < 0.5$ | Fair — 50–80%, *marginal* for critical tasks |
| $AI < 0.3$ | Poor — *not acceptable* for speech displays |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> Memorise this table for the 14-mark derivations. Examiner questions frequently hinge on SNR, AI, and the +15 dB speech rule.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in Design |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_{ref})$ | Sound Pressure Level | Convert measured pressure to dB |
| 2 | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_{ref})$ | Sound Intensity Level | Power-based dB scale |
| 3 | $L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}(\sum 10^{L_i/10})$ | Sum of uncorrelated sources | Combine multiple display tones |
| 4 | $SNR = L_{signal} - L_{noise}$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | Detectability check |
| 5 | $L_{dB(A)} = L_{dB} - A(f)$ | A-weighted SPL | Hearing-damage / ambient noise metric |
| 6 | $AI = \sum W_i \cdot [(S_i - N_i + 15)/30]$ | Articulation Index | Speech-display intelligibility |
| 7 | $L_{2} = L_{1} - 20\log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ | Inverse-square law | Predict level at distance |
| 8 | $f_{eff} = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$ | Geometric centre frequency | Band tuning |
| 9 | $\theta = \arcsin(\Delta t \cdot c / d)$ | Localisation cue | Spatial audio design |
| 10 | $T_{RT} = 0.161 \cdot V / A$ | Sabine's reverberation | Room acoustics for displays |

**Legend:** $p_{ref}=20\,\mu$Pa; $I_{ref}=10^{-12}$ W/m²; $c \approx 343$ m/s (speed of sound at 20°C); $V$ = room volume; $A$ = total absorption (sabins).

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Avionics & cockpits:** Heads-Up Displays are backed by *Master Caution* and *voice aural alerts* (e.g., "TERRAIN, TERRAIN, PULL UP").
- **Medical devices:** Pulse-oximeter beep rate encodes heart rate; infusion pumps emit *speech alarms* for occlusion.
- **Industrial control rooms:** SCADA systems use earcons to flag abnormal PLC tags when the operator's eyes are on a paper logbook.
- **Automotive ADAS:** Forward-collision warnings use *spatial earcons* (left/right channel) to direct driver attention.
- **Assistive technology:** Screen readers (JAWS, NVDA) are *speech audio displays* for the visually impaired.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Required Display Level to Overcome Industrial Noise

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (typical KTU 14-mark setup):** A factory floor has broadband ambient noise of $L_N = 85$ dB SPL (A-weighted). Design a non-speech audio display (complex signal) for an emergency alarm. Compute the *minimum display level* and the *required sound pressure in Pa*.

### Step 1 — Identify the masking requirement

For a *complex* (non-pure-tone) signal, the minimum SNR for audibility under typical industrial noise is $\Delta L_{min} = 6$ dB (see Section 2.2).

### Step 2 — Compute the minimum signal level

$$L_{S,min} = L_N + \Delta L_{min}$$

$$L_{S,min} = 85 + 6 = 91 \text{ dB SPL}$$

### Step 3 — Add the safety margin for high-criticality alarms

For life-safety alarms, ISO 7731 recommends a margin of at least **+15 dB** above ambient for *reliable detection without training*:

$$L_{S,design} = L_N + 15 = 85 + 15 = 100 \text{ dB SPL}$$

### Step 4 — Convert dB SPL to Pascals

$$\begin{aligned}
L_p &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_{ref}}\right) \\[4pt]
\frac{p}{p_{ref}} &= 10^{L_p/20} \\[4pt]
p &= p_{ref} \cdot 10^{L_p/20} \\[4pt]
p &= 20 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10^{100/20} \\[4pt]
p &= 20 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10^{5} \\[4pt]
p &= 20 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 100{,}000 \\[4pt]
\boxed{p &= 2.0 \text{ Pa}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The loudspeaker must produce a sound pressure of at least **2 Pa (rms)** at the operator's ear — roughly the level of a loud shout at 1 m. This must also be sustained for a *minimum duration* of 0.5 s to be reliably detected.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Articulation Index for a Speech Display

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A cockpit voice warning system operates in octave-band noise. Compute the AI for the data given, and decide if the message will be intelligible.

**Given Octave-Band Data:**

| Band Centre (Hz) | $W_i$ | Speech $S_i$ (dB) | Noise $N_i$ (dB) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 500 | 0.12 | 60 | 62 |
| 1000 | 0.16 | 62 | 64 |
| 2000 | 0.18 | 60 | 62 |
| 4000 | 0.14 | 55 | 60 |

### Step 1 — Compute band contribution

The contribution of each band to the AI is:

$$c_i = W_i \cdot \frac{(S_i - N_i + 15)}{30}$$

**Band 500 Hz:**

$$c_1 = 0.12 \cdot \frac{60 - 62 + 15}{30} = 0.12 \cdot \frac{13}{30} = 0.12 \cdot 0.433 = 0.0520$$

**Band 1000 Hz:**

$$c_2 = 0.16 \cdot \frac{62 - 64 + 15}{30} = 0.16 \cdot \frac{13}{30} = 0.16 \cdot 0.433 = 0.0693$$

**Band 2000 Hz:**

$$c_3 = 0.18 \cdot \frac{60 - 62 + 15}{30} = 0.18 \cdot 0.433 = 0.0780$$

**Band 4000 Hz:**

$$c_4 = 0.14 \cdot \frac{55 - 60 + 15}{30} = 0.14 \cdot \frac{10}{30} = 0.14 \cdot 0.333 = 0.0467$$

### Step 2 — Sum contributions for total AI

$$\begin{aligned}
AI &= c_1 + c_2 + c_3 + c_4 \\[4pt]
AI &= 0.0520 + 0.0693 + 0.0780 + 0.0467 \\[4pt]
\boxed{AI &= 0.246}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

Since $AI = 0.246 < 0.3$, intelligibility is **POOR** and *not acceptable* for critical cockpit warnings. The speech display would need either (a) **+6 dB louder speech levels** or (b) **noise reduction in the 2 kHz band** to push the AI above 0.5.

**Verification (sanity check):** Each $S_i - N_i$ is only $-2$ dB. To raise the AI to 0.5, $S_i - N_i$ would need to rise by ~6 dB across the bands — confirming the engineering recommendation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Inverse-Square Law for Display Coverage

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A paging-tone speaker rated at 95 dB SPL at 1 m is to be mounted at a workstation. Verify whether it remains $\geq 10$ dB above ambient at the operator's seat 4 m away.

### Step 1 — Apply inverse-square level drop

$$\begin{aligned}
L_2 &= L_1 - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) \\[4pt]
L_2 &= 95 - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{1}\right) \\[4pt]
L_2 &= 95 - 20 \cdot 0.6021 \\[4pt]
L_2 &= 95 - 12.04 \\[4pt]
L_2 &= 82.96 \text{ dB SPL}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Compute SNR at the seat

$$SNR = L_2 - L_N = 82.96 - 75 = 7.96 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 3 — Decision

Since $SNR \approx 8 \text{ dB} < 10$ dB, the design **fails** the audibility criterion. **Recommendation:** either (a) relocate the speaker to $\leq 2.5$ m, or (b) use a speaker rated at $\geq 103$ dB SPL at 1 m.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing an Audio Display Type

| Design Criterion | Auditory Icon | Earcon | Speech Display |
|---|---|---|---|
| Learning time | Short (familiar sound) | Long (must learn code) | Short (natural language) |
| Information density | Low | Medium | High |
| Multilingual operation | Yes | Yes | **No** (translation required) |
| Effectiveness in noise | Low | Medium | Medium-High (if $AI \geq 0.5$) |
| Cognitive load on operator | Low | Medium | **High** (must listen to whole message) |
| Best use case | Discrete events | Status indicators | Complex, variable warnings |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Classification Hierarchy of Audio Displays

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[AUDIO DISPLAYS] --> B[Non-Speech Audio Displays]
    A --> C[Speech Audio Displays]
    B --> B1[Auditory Icons]
    B --> B2[Earcons]
    B --> B3[Audification]
    B1 --> B1a[Nominal Mapping]
    B1 --> B1b[Symbolic Mapping]
    B2 --> B2a[One-Element Earcon]
    B2 --> B2b[Compound Earcon]
    B2 --> B2c[Family Earcon]
    C --> C1[Recorded Speech]
    C --> C2[Synthesized Speech TTS]
    C1 --> C1a[Fixed Vocabulary]
    C1 --> C1b[Concatenative Phrases]
    C2 --> C2a[Formant Synthesis]
    C2 --> C2b[Neural TTS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block-diagram maps the conceptual hierarchy of audio display types without attempting a physical schematic, which would be inappropriate for an abstract taxonomy.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Audio Display Design Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Step 1 Identify Information Need] --> stepB[Step 2 Determine Display Type Icon Earcon or Speech]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Choose Centre Frequency in 1 to 4 kHz Range]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Measure Ambient Noise Spectrum]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Compute Required SNR]
    stepE --> stepF{Step 6 SNR Acceptable above 10 dB}
    stepF -- Yes --> stepG[Step 7 Define Loudness Level and Duration]
    stepF -- No --> stepH[Step 8 Redesign Frequency or Use Earcon in Quiet Band]
    stepH --> stepC
    stepG --> stepI[Step 9 Prototype and User Test]
    stepI --> stepJ{Step 10 Intelligibility above 80 percent}
    stepJ -- Yes --> stepK[Step 11 Deploy Display]
    stepJ -- No --> stepL[Step 12 Iterate on Design]
    stepL --> stepC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the flow:** The dashed/decision blocks (stepF, stepJ) implement two critical feedback loops. In real engineering practice, design *never* terminates at first prototype — at least one iteration is mandatory, especially for life-safety systems (IEC 60601-1-8, ISO 7731).

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Audio Display Signal-Processing Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[Information Source Machine State] --> node2[Encoder Maps State to Acoustic Parameters]
    node2 --> node3[Signal Generator D A Converter and Amplifier]
    node3 --> node4[Acoustic Channel Room Air Headphones]
    node4 --> node5[Human Ear Outer Middle Inner Cochlea]
    node5 --> node6[Auditory Nerve and Cortex]
    node6 --> node7[Perceived Meaning Operator Response]
```

**Functional description of each block:**

| Block | Function | Design Variables |
|---|---|---|
| Information Source | Binary/multi-state machine output | Bits to encode |
| Encoder | Maps each state to a unique tone/sound | Pitch, rhythm, timbre |
| Signal Generator | Produces acoustic waveform | Frequency, amplitude, envelope |
| Acoustic Channel | Path from source to ear | SNR, reverberation, attenuation |
| Human Ear | Transducer (acoustic → neural) | Age, hearing profile |
| Auditory Nerve | Neural encoding | Lateralization (left/right) |
| Cortex | Pattern recognition | Training, expectation |

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Critical-Band Masking Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    m1[Masker Tone at Frequency f0] --> m2[Critical Bandwidth around f0 approximately one-third octave]
    m2 --> m3[Masks All Signals Inside the Critical Band]
    m2 --> m4[Raises Threshold by 3 to 24 dB depending on Level]
    m3 --> m5[Design Implication Move Display Frequency Outside this Band]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define an audio display. List the four psychoacoustic parameters used to encode information in a non-speech audio signal.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Audio Display:** A human-machine interface device that conveys operational information to the user through the auditory channel, using either structured non-speech sounds (tones, melodies) or speech messages.
>
> The four psychoacoustic parameters are:
> 1. **Frequency (Pitch)** — encodes identity / type of event
> 2. **Amplitude (Loudness)** — encodes urgency / magnitude
> 3. **Duration (Temporal pattern)** — encodes priority / status change
> 4. **Timbre / Spectral content** — encodes source identity

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**What is the Articulation Index (AI) and what is its minimum acceptable value for a safety-critical speech display?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Articulation Index (AI)** is a weighted-SNR metric that predicts the fraction of speech items (phonemes, words, sentences) correctly understood by a listener in a given noise environment. It is computed as:
>
> $AI = \sum_i W_i \cdot [(S_i - N_i + 15)/30]$
>
> For **safety-critical** speech displays (aviation, medical, nuclear control), the minimum acceptable AI is **0.5** (≈ 80% word intelligibility). Below 0.3, the display is considered unsuitable.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **masking phenomenon** with reference to **critical-band theory**. How does it influence the choice of centre frequency for an industrial alarm? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital ICU monitor is to alert staff using a speech display. The ambient noise level at the nurses' station is 68 dB(A), with octave-band noise peaks at 1 kHz (62 dB) and 2 kHz (60 dB). Compute the **minimum AI** and recommend whether the speech message will be reliable. Given importance weights $W_{500}=0.10$, $W_{1000}=0.16$, $W_{2000}=0.20$, $W_{4000}=0.12$, and assumed speech levels $S_i = N_i + 8$ dB in every band. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Masking Phenomenon:**

Masking is the process by which the perception of one sound (the *signal*) is reduced or eliminated by the simultaneous presence of another (the *masker*). It arises from the **mechanical filtering action of the basilar membrane** in the cochlea.

**Critical-Band Theory (Zwicker, 1961):**

- The basilar membrane behaves as a **bank of ~24 overlapping band-pass filters** across the audible range.
- Each filter has a bandwidth called a *critical band* (~⅓ octave wide above 500 Hz).
- A tone is masked only if its frequency lies *inside the critical band of the masker*.
- The **masked threshold** rises by 3–24 dB above the masker level depending on band proximity.

**Influence on Alarm Frequency:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Industrial noise has dominant energy below 500 Hz. To minimise masking, the alarm's centre frequency must be placed in the **2–4 kHz band**, where:
> 1. Ear sensitivity is maximal (~0 dB HL).
> 2. Critical bands are narrower, reducing overlap with low-frequency maskers.
> 3. Background noise energy is typically 10–15 dB lower.

**Design rule:** Centre frequency = **2.5 kHz**, bandwidth ≤ **½ octave**, level ≥ ambient + 15 dB.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $L_N = 68$ dB(A); $S_i - N_i = +8$ dB in every band.

**Step 1 — Identify $S_i - N_i$:**

In every band, $S_i - N_i = +8$ dB.

**Step 2 — Compute contribution per band:**

The formula gives:

$$c_i = W_i \cdot \frac{(8 + 15)}{30} = W_i \cdot \frac{23}{30} = W_i \cdot 0.7667$$

**Step 3 — Apply the weights:**

$$\begin{aligned}
c_{500} &= 0.10 \cdot 0.7667 = 0.0767 \\
c_{1000} &= 0.16 \cdot 0.7667 = 0.1227 \\
c_{2000} &= 0.20 \cdot 0.7667 = 0.1533 \\
c_{4000} &= 0.12 \cdot 0.7667 = 0.0920
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Sum for total AI:**

$$\begin{aligned}
AI &= 0.0767 + 0.1227 + 0.1533 + 0.0920 \\
\boxed{AI &= 0.4447 \approx 0.44}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**

Since $0.3 \leq AI < 0.5$, intelligibility is **Fair** (≈ 50–80% word recognition). The speech display is **acceptable for routine status messages but NOT for life-critical alarms**. For critical ICU alerts, augment the speech with a *distinctive earcon* (e.g., triple beep at 2.5 kHz) so that even partial intelligibility triggers recognition.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Distinguish between **auditory icons** and **earcons** with examples. Discuss the ergonomic criteria for selecting between them in an industrial control room. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory operator sits 3 m from an alarm speaker. The speaker produces 92 dB SPL at 1 m. The ambient noise is 80 dB(A). **(i)** Calculate the SPL at the operator's position. **(ii)** Determine if the alarm meets the 10 dB SNR criterion. **(iii)** If not, suggest two engineering remedies. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Feature | Auditory Icon | Earcon |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Naturalistic everyday sound mapped to event | Abstract musical motif mapped to event |
| **Origin** | Term: William Gaver, 1986 | Term: Blattner et al., 1989 |
| **Learning curve** | Low (uses pre-existing knowledge) | High (must memorise mapping) |
| **Example** | *Thud* sound → "file deleted" | Three ascending notes → "level up" |
| **Cognitive load** | Low | Medium-High initially |
| **Universality** | Cross-cultural, but loudness varies | Cross-cultural if motif is simple |
| **Best for** | Discrete, real-world events | Abstract / system-internal events |

**Ergonomic Selection Criteria for an Industrial Control Room:**

> [!TIP]
> Use **auditory icons** when a *natural physical analogy* exists (e.g., a *metal-clang* for a pressure-vessel over-pressure). Use **earcons** for *system-internal events* that have no real-world sound (e.g., a database transaction, a network timeout).
>
> Combined *hybrid* displays (icon + speech label) are recommended for high-stakes events.

**Specific recommendations:**

1. **Operator familiarity** — Auditory icons need no training; earcons require a "legend card."
2. **Number of distinct signals** — If $> 6$ signals are needed, earcons are easier to compose systematically.
3. **Noise environment** — Icons are best when matched to the dominant noise's spectrum.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) SPL at 3 m:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_2 &= L_1 - 20 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1) \\
L_2 &= 92 - 20 \log_{10}(3/1) \\
L_2 &= 92 - 20(0.4771) \\
L_2 &= 92 - 9.54 \\
\boxed{L_2 &= 82.46 \text{ dB SPL}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) SNR check:**

$$SNR = L_2 - L_N = 82.46 - 80 = 2.46 \text{ dB}$$

Since $2.46 < 10$ dB, the alarm **FAILS** the audibility criterion.

**(iii) Two Engineering Remedies:**

1. **Reposition the speaker closer** — moving it to $r_2 = 1$ m raises the level to $92$ dB, giving $SNR = 12$ dB ✓.
2. **Use a more powerful speaker** — selecting one rated at $L_1 \geq 100$ dB at 1 m gives $L_2 = 90.46$ dB and $SNR = 10.46$ dB ✓.
3. **Shift the alarm frequency** into the 2–4 kHz band to bypass low-frequency noise masking (alternative remedy).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks lost on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "+15 dB"** in the AI formula — students often write $(S_i - N_i)/30$ and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing dB SPL with dB(A)** — dB(A) is a *weighted* value used for hearing-damage assessment, not for raw signal-detection calculations.
> 3. **Mixing up inverse-square and inverse-cube laws** — sound pressure falls as $1/r^2$ in free field, *not* $1/r$.
> 4. **Not validating against the 0.5 AI threshold** for safety-critical speech — mere computation without a recommendation forfeits the "engineering judgment" mark.
> 5. **Omitting units** in the formula sheet — losing ½ mark per missing unit is a frequent silent killer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Audio display =** auditory interface encoding information via sound; subclass of *non-visual displays*.
- [ ] **Four parameters:** Pitch, Loudness, Duration, Timbre.
- [ ] **Reference pressure:** $p_{ref} = 20 \,\mu$Pa; intensity reference $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W/m².
- [ ] **SPL formula:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p / p_{ref})$ dB.
- [ ] **Audibility rules:** Tones need +3 dB SNR; complex signals +6 dB; speech +10 to +15 dB.
- [ ] **Critical-band masking:** ~⅓ octave band; design tones in **2–4 kHz** band.
- [ ] **Articulation Index (AI):** $AI = \sum W_i \cdot [(S_i - N_i + 15)/30]$.
- [ ] **AI thresholds:** $\geq 0.7$ Excellent; 0.5–0.7 Good; 0.3–0.5 Fair; $<0.3$ Poor.
- [ ] **Auditory Icons** = naturalistic sounds (Gaver, 1986); **Earcons** = abstract motifs (Blattner, 1989).
- [ ] **Speech displays** support highest information density but are language-dependent and cognitive-load heavy.
- [ ] **Inverse-square law:** $L_2 = L_1 - 20 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ — always use for free-field coverage.
- [ ] **ISO 7731 / IEC 60601-1-8:** Standards governing alarm audibility in industrial / medical contexts.
- [ ] **Lifecycle of design:** Identify → Encode → Measure ambient → Compute SNR/AI → Prototype → Test → Iterate.
- [ ] **Always include engineering recommendation**, not just numerical answer — KTU rewards judgement.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Design of Audio Displays | KTU 2024 Scheme Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT864 - ERGONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control (Ergonomics Definition):** A *control* is any device, mechanism, or interface element by which a human operator deliberately initiates, modifies, or terminates a functional state of a machine, process, or system. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a control is the **transmitting element** in the *Human–Machine Information Loop* — it is the physical embodiment of the operator's intent.

Controls are the **output side** of the human operator and the **input side** of the machine. They receive *motor energy* (force, displacement) from the user and convert it into a *signal* (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, mechanical) that the machine can interpret.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — Driving a Car:**
> Think of a car as the machine and you (the driver) as the operator. The **steering wheel**, **accelerator pedal**, **brake pedal**, and **gear shift lever** are all *controls*. When you turn the steering wheel, you are sending a precise rotational command to the front wheels. When you press the brake pedal, you are transmitting a *force* that the hydraulic system converts into stopping power. Just as a poorly placed accelerator pedal can cause accidents, a poorly designed control leads to **operator error, fatigue, and reduced system performance**. The job of an ergonomist is to design these interfaces so that the driver's *intent* is transmitted with **minimum effort, maximum accuracy, and zero ambiguity**.

## 1.2 Classification of Controls (Foundation of KTU Module 4)

Controls are broadly classified along two primary dimensions:

| Classification Axis | Categories | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **By Function** | Discrete (binary) | Push-buttons, toggle switches, rotary selector switches |
| | Continuous (proportional) | Steering wheels, joysticks, accelerator pedals, throttle levers |
| **By Actuation Mode** | Mechanical | Levers, cranks, knobs, wheels |
| | Electronic | Touch-screens, capacitive keypads, trackballs |
| | Pneumatic / Hydraulic | Brake pedals, hydraulic valves |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly demands that students distinguish between **discrete** and **continuous** controls and apply the *NATO Standardization Agreement (STANAG)* and *MIL-STD-1472* design principles when justifying control selection.

### 1.3 The Human–Machine Control Loop

The control is positioned at the critical junction where **human motor output** becomes **machine input**. The flow is:

1. Operator forms **intent** (cognitive stage).
2. CNS generates **motor command** (neuromuscular stage).
3. Muscles actuate the **control** (physical interface).
4. Control transmits a **signal** to the machine.
5. Machine responds; **feedback** (display) closes the loop.

A well-designed control optimizes **stages 3 and 4** — minimizing effort, error, and time.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Industrial Values)

- **Control Force (F):** typically **20 N to 100 N** for hand-operated levers; **5 N to 50 N** for finger-operated push-buttons.
- **Displacement Range:** Rotary controls: **30° to 120°**; Linear controls: **25 mm to 150 mm**.
- **Resistance:** Critical for feedback — must be ≥ **5 N** for distinct detent feel.
- **Operation Time (T):** < **0.5 s** for emergency discrete controls (per MIL-STD-1472).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Discrete (Binary) Controls — Design Logic

Discrete controls have **two (or a few) stable states**: ON/OFF, START/STOP, UP/DOWN. They are used when the operator's intent is *categorical*, not *proportional*.

### 2.1.1 Push-Buttons (Most Common Discrete Control)

A push-button is actuated by a **single-digit press** (usually the index finger or thumb). The design parameters that govern its ergonomic quality are:

1. **Button Diameter (D):** Recommended **10 mm to 25 mm** for finger operation. Too small → missed actuations; too large → slow operation.
2. **Actuation Force (F):** Recommended **2.5 N to 5 N** for finger; **10 N to 20 N** for palm/hand operation.
3. **Travel (Stroke, S):** **3 mm to 6 mm** for tactile feel.
4. **Surface Texture:** Matte finish recommended to prevent slippage from sweaty fingers.
5. **Color Coding:** Red = STOP/EMERGENCY; Green = START; Yellow = CAUTION; Blue = INFORMATIONAL (per ANSI Z535.1 and ISO 3864).

### 2.1.2 Toggle Switches

Toggle switches are **two-position or three-position** levers pivoted at one end. They provide excellent *tactile feedback* and *visual state indication*.

- **Toggle Length (L):** **10 mm to 50 mm** depending on force requirements.
- **Pivot Position:** At one end (cantilever) for finger; at center (teeter-totter) for balanced thumb operation.
- **Deflection Angle:** **30° to 60°** from neutral.
- **Resistance Force:** **2 N to 8 N**.

### 2.1.3 Rotary Selector Switches

Used when more than two discrete states must be selected (e.g., 3-speed fan, 4-mode camera dial). They are *continuous-in-construction but discrete-in-function* (detent positions).

- **Detent Spacing:** **15° to 30°** between adjacent positions.
- **Number of Positions:** **2 to 12** (beyond 12, use a numeric keypad instead).
- **Knob Diameter:** **15 mm to 50 mm**.

## 2.2 Continuous (Proportional) Controls — Design Logic

Continuous controls allow **infinitely variable** input. The output is proportional to the operator's input displacement. They are used for steering, throttling, aiming, and tracking tasks.

### 2.2.1 Rotary Continuous Controls (Steering Wheels, Knobs)

The relationship between operator hand input and machine output is governed by the **Control Ratio (CR)**:

$$
CR = \frac{\theta_{out}}{\theta_{in}}
$$

where $\theta_{in}$ is the angular displacement of the operator's hand and $\theta_{out}$ is the corresponding angular displacement of the controlled element (e.g., front wheels of a car).

- **Low CR (< 1):** Highly sensitive — small input → large output. Used in **precision tracking** (joystick for surgical robot).
- **High CR (> 1):** Less sensitive — requires large input motion. Used in **heavy machinery steering** (forklift, ship wheel).
- **CR = 1 (1:1 ratio):** Direct, intuitive coupling (e.g., fly-by-wire gaming wheel in simulation mode).

### 2.2.2 Linear Continuous Controls (Levers, Pedals, Sliders)

For linear controls, the analogous metric is the **Mechanical Advantage (MA)**:

$$
MA = \frac{F_{in}}{F_{out}} = \frac{d_{out}}{d_{in}}
$$

where $F_{in}$ is the operator's applied force, $F_{out}$ is the output force at the load, and $d$ represents displacements. This is a direct application of the **conservation of energy** (neglecting friction).

### 2.2.3 Cranks and Handwheels

Cranks convert **rotary motion** into **linear oscillating motion**. The design parameter is the **crank radius (R)**, which determines the torque requirement and the swept-finger clearance.

- **Crank Radius:** Typically **30 mm to 100 mm**.
- **Optimal Rotation Speed:** **30 to 60 rpm** (above this, centripetal acceleration on the hand becomes uncomfortable).

The **hand clearance zone** (the volume swept by the hand during rotation) must be free of obstructions. For a crank of radius R, the clearance cylinder has a diameter of approximately:

$$
D_{clearance} = 2R + 2W_{hand}
$$

where $W_{hand} \approx 90 \text{ mm}$ is the average metacarpal hand width.

## 2.3 The Control–Display Ratio (C/D Ratio) — *CRITICAL KTU CONCEPT*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control–Display Ratio (C/D Ratio):** The ratio of control movement to display movement. It is a *gain* parameter that links the operator's motor action to the visual feedback they observe.

$$
C/D = \frac{\text{Movement of Control element}}{\text{Movement of Display element}}
$$

### 2.2.1 Sub-ratios (Welford's Decomposition)

Welford decomposed the overall C/D ratio into three multiplicative sub-ratios:

$$
(C/D)_{overall} = (C/D)_{A} \times (C/D)_{B} \times (C/D)_{C}
$$

where:
- **$(C/D)_A$** = Movement of control linkage / Movement of control operated by hand (*Control-to-Linkage*).
- **$(C/D)_B$** = Movement of machine element / Movement of control linkage (*Linkage-to-Machine*).
- **$(C/D)_C$** = Movement of display / Movement of machine element (*Machine-to-Display*).

### 2.2.2 Design Implications

| C/D Range | Effect | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Very Low (< 0.1)** | Coarse control, large display movement, small control movement | Quick gross adjustments, e.g., camera framing |
| **Low (0.1 – 1.0)** | Fine control, amplified display response | Precision tracking, e.g., aircraft trim |
| **Unity (1.0)** | 1:1 correspondence | Direct manipulation interfaces |
| **High (> 1.0)** | Display moves less than control — used when display space is limited | Industrial gauges with limited dial size |

## 2.4 Population Stereotypes (Cultural-Predictive Design)

> [!NOTE]
> **Population Stereotype:** A learned, culturally-shared, *expectation* about the direction of response of a control. When the actual response matches the stereotype, reaction time is minimized and errors are reduced. When it *violates* the stereotype, performance degrades sharply.

### 2.4.1 Universal Stereotypes (from KSU / Loveless / Smith studies)

| Control Type | Expected Effect | Universal Direction |
|---|---|---|
| Linear lever (fore-aft) | Move up → something increases | **Move UP = ON / UP / INCREASE** (~92% population) |
| Linear lever (fore-aft) | Pull toward body → something increases | **Pull TOWARD = INCREASE** (less universal) |
| Rotary knob (clock-face) | Clockwise rotation → something increases | **Clockwise = INCREASE / ON** (> 95% population) |
| Push-button (on horizontal panel) | Right button → something to the right increases | **Right Button = RIGHT Effect** (strong) |
| Push-button (vertical pair) | Top button → something increases | **Top Button = INCREASE / ON** (strong) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A classic 14-mark question asks students to **justify** the placement of accelerator (right pedal) versus brake (center/left pedal) in a car using population stereotypes and *foot-control* biomechanics. The right-foot dominance and *brake-on-the-left-from-accelerator* convention both align with universal stereotypes and reduce cross-foot errors.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Typical Value Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Control Ratio (rotary) | $CR = \theta_{out} / \theta_{in}$ | dimensionless | 0.1 – 10 |
| 2 | Mechanical Advantage (linear) | $MA = F_{in}/F_{out} = d_{out}/d_{in}$ | dimensionless | 0.1 – 10 |
| 3 | Overall C/D Ratio | $(C/D)_{ov} = (C/D)_A \cdot (C/D)_B \cdot (C/D)_C$ | dimensionless | 0.01 – 100 |
| 4 | Hand Clearance (crank) | $D_c = 2R + 2W_{hand}$ | mm | 150 – 300 mm |
| 5 | Push-button force | $F_{pb}$ | N | 2.5 – 5 (finger), 10 – 20 (palm) |
| 6 | Push-button travel | $S_{pb}$ | mm | 3 – 6 |
| 7 | Push-button diameter | $D_{pb}$ | mm | 10 – 25 |
| 8 | Lever grip diameter | $D_{grip}$ | mm | 30 – 50 |
| 9 | Maximum comfortable crank speed | $N_{max}$ | rpm | 30 – 60 |
| 10 | Emergency stop actuation time | $T_{estop}$ | s | < 0.5 |
| 11 | Reaction force (lever) | $F = T/L$ | N | 20 – 100 |
| 12 | Torque on rotary knob | $T = F \times R$ | N·m | 0.1 – 5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on units:** In KTU 2024 examination answers, *always* state the unit explicitly next to the numeric value. A value of "5" without "N" or "mm" is treated as incomplete by strict examiners.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design of controls is foundational to:

- **Automotive engineering:** Steering ratio selection, pedal placement, dashboard switch design.
- **Aerospace:** Yoke vs. sidestick choice, throttle quadrant layout in glass cockpits.
- **Medical devices:** Surgical robot joystick sensitivity, ventilator control ergonomics.
- **Industrial machinery:** CNC pendant design, forklift lever layouts.
- **Consumer electronics:** Smartphone touchscreen gesture design, gaming controller button placement.

Mastery of control design principles allows engineers to *predict* user behavior, *reduce* training time, *prevent* mode-confusion errors, and *comply* with international safety standards (ISO 9241, MIL-STD-1472, ANSI/HFES 100).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mechanical Advantage of a Class-2 Lever Control

Consider a typical foot-pedal control (e.g., brake pedal) modeled as a **Class-2 lever**. The fulcrum is at one end (pivot at the firewall), the operator applies force $F_{in}$ at the pedal pad, and the output force $F_{out}$ acts on the master-cylinder pushrod near the fulcrum.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We start with the principle of **static moment equilibrium** about the pivot:

$$
\sum M_{pivot} = 0
$$

The operator's input force $F_{in}$ acts at a distance $L_{in}$ from the pivot (the long arm of the lever). The output force $F_{out}$ acts at a distance $L_{out}$ from the pivot (the short arm connected to the master cylinder).

Setting the clockwise and counter-clockwise moments equal:

$$
F_{in} \cdot L_{in} = F_{out} \cdot L_{out}
$$

Solving for the mechanical advantage (defined as the ratio of output force to input force):

$$
MA = \frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = \frac{L_{in}}{L_{out}}
$$

This is the *ideal* mechanical advantage. In practice, friction in the pivot introduces a small loss, so the *actual* MA is slightly lower. The **efficiency** $\eta$ is defined as:

$$
\eta = \frac{F_{out, \text{actual}}}{F_{out, \text{ideal}}} = \frac{F_{out, \text{actual}} \cdot L_{out}}{F_{in} \cdot L_{in}}
$$

### Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

A brake pedal has $L_{in} = 200$ mm and $L_{out} = 40$ mm. The operator applies a foot force of 150 N. Calculate the ideal output force on the master cylinder and the mechanical advantage.

**Step 1:** Calculate mechanical advantage.

$$
MA = \frac{L_{in}}{L_{out}} = \frac{200 \text{ mm}}{40 \text{ mm}} = 5
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the ideal output force.

$$
F_{out} = MA \cdot F_{in} = 5 \times 150 \text{ N} = 750 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation. The pedal multiplies the operator's leg force by a factor of 5. The trade-off: the operator's foot must move 5× the distance of the master-cylinder rod.

$$
d_{in} = MA \cdot d_{out} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_{out} = \frac{d_{in}}{MA}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** A common error in KTU answer scripts is to invert the lever arms. Always confirm which arm the input force acts on. The longer arm corresponds to lower input force (higher MA) but greater input displacement.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Control–Display Ratio Decomposition for a Steering System

Consider a car's steering system. The driver rotates the steering wheel through an angle $\theta_{wheel}$ (e.g., 90° = quarter turn). The front wheels respond by rotating through $\theta_{wheel,out}$ (e.g., 4.5°). A steering ratio of 20:1 is typical for passenger cars.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The **overall Control–Display ratio** for the steering system is:

$$
(C/D)_{overall} = \frac{\theta_{hand}}{\theta_{wheel,out}}
$$

where $\theta_{hand}$ is the rotation of the driver's hand on the wheel.

In Welford's decomposition, the chain of transformations is:

**Step A — Hand to wheel rim:** The hand directly drives the wheel rim (1:1). There is no linkage here in a typical car.

$$
(C/D)_A = \frac{\theta_{wheel}}{\theta_{hand}} = 1
$$

**Step B — Wheel rim to pinion/road wheel:** The steering column drives a pinion gear, which moves a rack or pitman arm, which rotates the road wheel. This is the **steering gearbox ratio**.

$$
(C/D)_B = \frac{\theta_{wheel}}{\theta_{pinion}} = 20
$$

**Step C — Road wheel to display feedback:** The driver observes the *change in car direction* (yaw), not just the wheel angle. The display is the visual scene.

$$
(C/D)_C = \frac{\theta_{yaw}}{\theta_{wheel}}
$$

For a passenger car, a typical yaw response is $\theta_{yaw} \approx 0.1 \times \theta_{wheel}$ for small angles. So:

$$
(C/D)_C = 0.1
$$

**Combined overall ratio:**

$$
(C/D)_{overall} = (C/D)_A \times (C/D)_B \times (C/D)_C = 1 \times 20 \times 0.1 = 2
$$

This means: for every 1° the driver's hand turns, the *display* (visual scene) effectively moves 2° (in equivalent angular terms). This is a "high gain" system, suitable for highway speeds where gross corrections are sufficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Connection:** A typical 14-mark question asks students to *redesign* a steering ratio for a *low-speed parking maneuver* and *justify* the change using C/D ratio logic. The answer should reduce $(C/D)_B$ to around 12:1 (or use a variable-ratio rack) to allow finer control at low speeds.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Push-Button Actuation Force and Hand Biomechanics

A push-button is operated by the **distal phalanx of the index finger**. The comfortable force range is bounded by two biomechanical limits:

- **Lower bound (F_min):** If the force is too low, *accidental actuations* occur (e.g., pocket dialing, vibration-induced presses). The recommended minimum is **2.5 N**.
- **Upper bound (F_max):** If the force is too high, *finger fatigue* and *slow operation* result. The recommended maximum is **5 N** for index-finger operation, or **2.8 N** for thumb-only operation (thumb has ~30% the strength of index finger).

The **moment arm** from the finger's interphalangeal joint to the button surface is approximately $r = 25$ mm. The required **flexor tendon tension** $T_{tendon}$ to produce button force $F_{button}$ is:

$$
T_{tendon} = \frac{F_{button} \cdot r}{d_{moment}}
$$

where $d_{moment} \approx 15$ mm is the perpendicular distance from the tendon to the joint center. Plugging in numbers for $F_{button} = 5$ N:

$$
T_{tendon} = \frac{5 \text{ N} \times 25 \text{ mm}}{15 \text{ mm}} = 8.33 \text{ N}
$$

This tendon tension is well within the index-finger's flexor strength (which can exceed 100 N for a healthy adult), so the button feels "easy" but "deliberate" — the hallmark of good ergonomic design.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Control Selection Decision Table

The following decision matrix summarizes how to select the *type* of control based on the task requirement. This is a high-yield KTU examination tool.

| Task Characteristic | Recommended Control Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Binary on/off, fast activation | Large push-button or mushroom-head button | Maximum surface area, minimum actuation time |
| Multi-state selection (2–4) | Toggle switch | Clear visual + tactile state indication |
| Multi-state selection (4–12) | Rotary selector with detents | Limited hand travel, distinct positions |
| Continuous proportional tracking (precision) | Joystick, small-displacement knob | High resolution, low force |
| Continuous proportional tracking (power) | Large steering wheel, foot pedal | High mechanical advantage, low operator fatigue |
| Emergency stop | Mushroom-head palm button, red | Largest target, highest force, fail-safe |
| Sequential menu navigation | Membrane keypad or touch-screen | Programmable, low cost |
| Spatial positioning (3-D) | 3-axis joystick or space mouse | Decoupled axes for X, Y, Z control |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** When asked to "design a control panel for X," always begin with a **task analysis** (what does the operator do?), then a **control selection matrix** (which control type per task?), then **layout (population-stereotype-driven)**, and finally **force/displacement specification (anthropometry-driven)**. This 4-step structure is the hallmark of a high-scoring answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Human–Machine Control Loop

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow of information from operator intent through the control interface to the machine and back via the display. This replaces what would otherwise be a hand-drawn feedback-loop diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operator Intent Cognitive Planning] --> B[CNS Motor Command Neuromuscular Stage]
    B --> C[Muscle Actuation Physical Force]
    C --> D[Control Device Pushbutton Lever Wheel]
    D --> E[Linkage Mechanism Gear Lever Cable]
    E --> F[Machine Element Throttle Valve Brake Cylinder]
    F --> G[Process State Vehicle Motion Tool Path]
    G --> H[Display Visual Auditory Tactile Feedback]
    H --> A
    D -.->|Force F N| D
    F -.->|Displacement d mm| F
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Ergonomic Controls

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Controls] --> B[By Actuation Mode]
    A --> C[By Signal Type]
    A --> D[By Function]
    B --> B1[Mechanical]
    B --> B2[Electronic]
    B --> B3[Pneumatic Hydraulic]
    C --> C1[Discrete Binary]
    C --> C2[Continuous Proportional]
    D --> D1[Pushbutton]
    D --> D2[Toggle Switch]
    D --> D3[Rotary Selector]
    D --> D4[Steering Wheel]
    D --> D5[Joystick]
    D --> D6[Foot Pedal]
    D --> D7[Crank Handwheel]
    C1 --> E1[Pushbutton]
    C1 --> E2[Toggle Switch]
    C2 --> E3[Joystick]
    C2 --> E4[Steering Wheel]
    C2 --> E5[Foot Pedal]
```

## 4.3 Control–Display Ratio Decomposition Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hand Movement theta hand] -->|CD sub A 1 to 1| B[Control Element Movement theta control]
    B -->|CD sub B Steering Ratio 20 to 1| C[Machine Element Movement theta machine]
    C -->|CD sub C Yaw Response 0 point 1| D[Display Movement theta display]
    D --> E[Overall CD Ratio Product of A B and C]
```

## 4.4 Control-Display Compatibility Layout Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Display A on Left] --> B[Control A on Left]
    C[Display B in Center] --> D[Control B in Center]
    E[Display C on Right] --> F[Control C on Right]
    G[Display Above] --> H[Control Above]
    I[Display Below] --> J[Control Below]
    K[Population Stereotype Right to Right Up to Up] --> B
    K --> D
    K --> F
    K --> H
    K --> J
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram of Push-Button Force Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Finger Applies Force F applied] --> B[Button Top Surface]
    B --> C[Spring Resistance F spring]
    B --> D[Switch Contact Closure F contact]
    B --> E[Tactile Feedback F tactile]
    C --> F[Net Actuation F net]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{Switch Closed?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Signal to Machine]
    G -->|No| I[No Action]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In SECTION 4.4, the side-by-side positioning of *Display-Left* with *Control-Left*, *Display-Center* with *Control-Center*, etc., visually encodes the **spatial-compatibility principle** (a sub-component of population stereotypes). When a student is asked in a 14-mark question to "redesign a control panel for an industrial mixer," this block diagram is the template they should follow.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "control" in ergonomics. Differentiate between discrete and continuous controls with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **control** is a device or mechanism by which a human operator initiates, modifies, or terminates a functional state of a machine or system. It is the *transmitting element* in the human–machine loop, converting the operator's motor output (force, displacement) into a machine-interpretable signal.

> **Discrete controls** have a finite, limited number of stable states (typically 2). The operator's intent is categorical. Examples: *push-button* (START/STOP), *toggle switch* (ON/OFF), *rotary selector* (3-speed fan dial).
